text: In the contemporary educational landscape, the approach to gifted education remains highly inconsistent across nations. While some countries have developed sophisticated, specialized programs to nurture high-potential students, others have yet to formally recognize giftedness as a distinct educational category. Typically, support for these children is facilitated through a patchwork of state schooling and external organizations. Despite these efforts, a persistent misconception remains—even among educators—that gifted programs are inherently elitist or that high-ability learners can succeed perfectly well without targeted intervention. Such myths create systemic barriers that hinder the realization of a child’s full potential. Without specialized attention and tailored pedagogical strategies, the unique talents of these students often remain unrecognized and underdeveloped. As part of a broader holistic study on gifted education, this article examines the diverse educational frameworks and support mechanisms currently employed across several European countries.
